network.overall_strategy: This network targets glycine through ten literature-grounded, convergent prebiotic routes centered on five hubs: hydrogen cyanide, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, and glycine itself. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks such as formate, formaldehyde, and pyruvate from CO2/H2 on Fe-S and Fe-oxide minerals under alkaline serpentinizing conditions. Surface environments generate and concentrate HCN, drive Strecker chemistry, Bucherer-Bergs diversion, cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry, and HCN polymer hydrolysis under wet-dry, UV, and eutectic concentration regimes. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slower hydrolyses, transductions, and oligomerization. The ten pathways are not isolated; they share intermediates and environmental transfer, with hydrothermal products feeding surface reactions and surface nitrile products feeding biochemical hydrolysis to glycine.

molecules.common_name: polymer hydrolysis amino acid precursor pool

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline serpentinizing conditions, H2-rich; formate reported up to 200 mM

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S/Fe-oxide catalytic sites generates surface-bound reduced C1 species released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 reduction to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions near 100C. This supplies a major hydrothermal carbon entry point consistent with serpentinization chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to formaldehyde-equivalent pool

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2/CO hydrogenation on vent-related mineral surfaces; treated as formaldehyde-equivalent feedstock generation rather than direct glycine synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Surface hydrogenation of oxidized carbon generates reduced carbonyl intermediates; formaldehyde is the relevant downstream aldehyde entering Strecker and cyanohydrin chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report identifies hydrothermal CO2-to-C1 reduction and FTT generation of aldehydes as environmentally plausible precursors, while cautioning against overstating direct FTT-to-glycine. Preiner et al. (2020) support reduced C1 formation in hydrothermal systems, and Yoshino et al. (1971) support catalyst-mediated FTT generation of Strecker precursors rather than direct amino acid single-step synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: greigite/magnetite-like Fe minerals; awaruite/Ni3Fe literature analog
conditions.other: alkaline conditions; pyruvate detected at ~10 uM at 100C and also at 25C under 25 bar with serpentinization-relevant catalysts

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and C-C coupling from reduced C1/C2 species yield alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2/H2 on hydrothermal minerals at 100C, and Beyazay et al. (2023) demonstrated room-temperature CO2 fixation to pyruvate on Ni3Fe nanoparticles at 25 bar. The system supports pyruvate as a hydrothermal hub feeding amino acid chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to glyoxylate oxidative-retroconversion pool

conditions.other: speculative network-connecting alpha-keto acid interconversion under anoxic hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid fragmentation/oxidation-rearrangement on iron-sulfur surfaces is invoked to connect pyruvate and glyoxylate pools.
reactions.reasoning: This step is included as a conservative connecting reaction to integrate the hydrothermal alpha-keto acid pool with glyoxylate-based glycine synthesis; direct pyruvate-to-glyoxylate conversion is less explicitly established in the provided literature and is therefore speculative. It is justified as a minimal connector between experimentally supported hydrothermal pyruvate production and experimentally supported glyoxylate-to-glycine synthesis. Relevant hydrothermal alpha-keto acid context comes from Preiner et al. (2020) and Beyazay et al. (2023); the speculative status is acknowledged.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide analog on Fe mineral surfaces
conditions.other: alkaline pH; maximum yield at approximately 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III)

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated imine formation and reduction converts the alpha-keto acid pyruvate into the alpha-amino acid alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (2019) demonstrated pyruvate + NH3 conversion to alanine using mixed-valence iron minerals under alkaline conditions. This hydrothermal route establishes alpha-keto acid to amino acid reactivity and provides an amino donor reservoir linked to later transamination logic.

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric/photochemical generation and concentration of HCN

conditions.other: UV/spark-driven atmospheric production followed by concentration in ice eutectic brines near -23.4C or in evaporitic settings; mineral surfaces aid photoredox handling and concentration rather than being sole source

reactions.mechanism: Energy input generates HCN from atmospheric carbon and nitrogen feedstocks; eutectic freezing and mineral-associated concentration raise local HCN above productive thresholds.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957), Schlesinger & Miller (1983), and Johnson et al. (2008) support atmospheric energy-driven generation of HCN-rich precursor mixtures, while Sanchez et al. (1966, 1967), Miyakawa et al. (2002), and Levy et al. (2000) show productive chemistry requires concentration, plausibly achieved by eutectic freezing. Surface mineral agents are included to satisfy the required mineral-catalyst schema and reflect photocatalytic/concentration roles described for TiO2 and sulfide minerals in prebiotic settings.

reactions.name: Surface Strecker condensation of formaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration on montmorillonite; photochemical setting compatible with TiO2-rich surfaces
conditions.other: pH near 8 in aqueous ponds or concentrated microenvironments; proceeds via aminomethanol and methanimine, with glycinonitrile formation barrier ~15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds to formaldehyde to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to methanimine; cyanide addition to the imine yields aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957) identified Strecker logic in spark-discharge amino acid synthesis, and Magrino et al. (2021) mapped the explicit aqueous free-energy landscape for formaldehyde + NH3 + HCN to aminoacetonitrile/glycinonitrile. This is the dominant prebiotic glycine route in the literature.

reactions.name: Surface cyanohydrin formation from formaldehyde and HCN to glycolonitrile

conditions.catalyst: borate mineral stabilization and TiO2-associated surface photochemistry
conditions.other: prebiotic aqueous solution with [HCN] and [HCHO] about 1 g/L; glycolonitrile strongly favored under low NH3 at pH 8

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to formaldehyde generates the cyanohydrin glycolonitrile, a major branch product and network connector.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) showed that realistic prebiotic HCN/HCHO systems at pH 8 overwhelmingly produce glycolonitrile (~99.9%) relative to aminoacetonitrile under low NH3, making glycolonitrile a necessary branch node in any glycine network.

reactions.name: Surface glycolonitrile ammonolysis to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite concentration and phosphate-buffered pond chemistry
conditions.other: low NH3 systems can still divert small aminoacetonitrile fractions through ammonolysis and concentration

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia substitutes into the cyanohydrin-derived intermediate pool to access the aminonitrile branch feeding glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) explicitly frame glycolonitrile and aminoacetonitrile as coupled products in prebiotic HCN/HCHO/NH3 systems, with aminoacetonitrile available in low but productive amounts. This reaction captures the Strecker-adjacent branch without inventing a new overall pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis; nitrile hydration barrier ~23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Water adds to the nitrile to form the corresponding amide, glycinamide, as the first hydrolytic intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) identified glycinamide as the required intermediate in aqueous Strecker hydrolysis, with nitrile hydration preceding final amide hydrolysis. This step is necessary for literature-faithful representation of the Strecker pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis; highest reported barrier in the Strecker hydrolysis sequence ~31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis converts glycinamide into the amino acid glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) showed the final amide hydrolysis to glycine is the rate-limiting step of the canonical Strecker route, with a barrier of about 31 kcal/mol and overall strongly exergonic formation of glycine.

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs capture of aminoacetonitrile by CO2 to hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: bicarbonate/CO2-rich buffered aqueous setting with mineral concentration
conditions.other: pH about 8; CO2 drives productive diversion of small aminoacetonitrile fractions toward hydantoin

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped in a carbonylation/cyclization sequence generating hydantoin as a more persistent reservoir intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) demonstrated that CO2 catalyzes productive conversion of aminoacetonitrile into hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions, making the Bucherer-Bergs branch a validated alternative route to glycine.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in concentrated prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of hydantoin yields the ureido amino acid intermediate N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades et al. (1998) describe hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine as part of the Bucherer-Bergs route to glycine under prebiotic conditions.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: decarbamoylating hydrolysis in aqueous prebiotic settings

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic loss of the carbamoyl group releases free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs sequence leading from aminoacetonitrile through hydantoin and N-carbamoylglycine to glycine is documented by Taillades et al. (1998).

reactions.name: Surface HCN oligomerization to DAMN/HCN polymer pool

conditions.catalyst: concentration on mineral surfaces during eutectic freezing or evaporative cycling
conditions.other: requires productive HCN concentration above ~0.01-0.1 M; dimerization rate measured at pH 9.2 and 40C; eutectic freezing near -23.4C can raise local HCN dramatically

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes base-promoted oligomerization through iminoacetonitrile, aminomalononitrile, and DAMN into polymeric nitrile-rich solids.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez et al. (1966, 1967) established the kinetics and threshold concentration behavior of HCN oligomerization, and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) showed that hydrolysis of the resulting polymers yields glycine abundantly. The polymer pool is therefore a validated independent glycine precursor class.

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of HCN polymer pool to amino acid precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: surface-assisted acid/base hydrolysis during wet-dry and UV cycles
conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis of HCN polymers in concentrated surface settings

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic opening and oxidation of nitrile-rich polymeric material yields amidated and carboxylated small-molecule precursors en route to amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid under all tested HCN polymer hydrolysis conditions, building on Oró-era HCN polymer chemistry. This step represents the established polymer-to-small-molecule transition.

reactions.name: Biochemical release of glycine from HCN polymer hydrolysate

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis and deprotection in concentrated pools

reactions.mechanism: Further hydrolysis and fragmentation of amino acid precursor mixtures releases free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) report glycine as the dominant amino acid released by HCN polymer hydrolysis across diverse conditions, consistent with the long-standing HCN polymer route initiated by Oró and later analyzed in systems studies.

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of aminoacetonitrile from HCN feedstock

conditions.catalyst: cyanosulfidic photoredox network on sulfide mineral-rich surfaces in phosphate buffer
conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S present as reductant; unified systems chemistry at about 35C

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes reductive homologation under UV-driven sulfur-based photoredox cycling, simultaneously generating sugar/aldehyde precursors and aminonitriles including aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) showed HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at 35C produces aminoacetonitrile and other biomolecule precursors in a unified cyanosulfidic network. This gives an independent literature-supported route to the glycine precursor aminoacetonitrile.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal ferroan-brucite mediated synthesis of glycine from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.other: pH 8-9 serpentinite-associated setting; up to 97% of produced glycine partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction of nitrate coupled to amination/reductive conversion of glyoxylate yields mineral-bound glycine.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) demonstrated ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate with strong mineral sequestration. This is one of the strongest direct hydrothermal routes to glycine in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: Surface/photocatalytic reversible amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.catalyst: UV-illuminated semiconductor mineral setting; literature analog ZnS photochemistry represented within required mineral schema by TiO2 plus sulfide surfaces
conditions.other: UV illumination; alpha-keto acid reversible amination chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces drive reductive amination of glyoxylate with ammonia to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) demonstrated UV-driven reversible amination of alpha-keto acids, including glyoxylate to glycine, on ZnS. The present network maps this surface photochemical logic into the required surface-mineral schema while preserving the literature-supported reaction type.

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination-like conversion of glyoxylate to glycine using alanine as amino donor

conditions.other: prebiotic pool transamination logic connecting hydrothermal alanine and glyoxylate pools

reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from alanine to glyoxylate yields glycine and regenerates pyruvate, increasing network closure around alpha-keto acid and amino acid hubs.
reactions.reasoning: The research report identifies transamination as part of metabolically relevant prebiotic chemistry and specifically highlights alpha-keto acids as hubs. This reaction is included as a conservative biochemical connector that ties the experimentally supported pyruvate/alanine hydrothermal branch (Barge et al., 2019) to glyoxylate-centered glycine synthesis. It is more speculative than Strecker or ferroan-brucite routes but chemically standard and network-connecting.

reactions.name: Surface formamide generation from HCN hydrolysis

conditions.other: minor branch; included only as literature-supported interconnection, not as a direct compressed glycine route

reactions.mechanism: Partial hydration of nitrile carbon in HCN yields formamide as a side-accessible C1 nitrogenous intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide chemistry appears in the supplied literature as part of UV/mineral prebiotic chemistry, but prior feedback correctly noted that direct formamide-to-glycine compression is unsupported. This reaction is therefore included only as an interconnection node and not used as a direct one-step glycine pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical oligomerization of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling or short hydrothermal pulses; dimerization optimum near pH 9.8 and 140C

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation forms a peptide bond between two glycine molecules, giving glycylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Sakata et al. (2010) defined the optimum for glycine dimerization near pH 9.8 and 140C, while Imai et al. (1999) demonstrated oligoglycine formation under hydrothermal flow. This reaction makes glycine an outgoing hub and connects synthesis to prebiotic polymer chemistry.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker pathway from hydrothermal formaldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formaldehyde, which undergoes surface Strecker condensation with ammonia and HCN to aminoacetonitrile, followed by two-step hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-assisted Strecker branch
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN first form glycolonitrile, which with ammonia feeds aminoacetonitrile and then canonical hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: The minor aminoacetonitrile fraction formed in Strecker chemistry is captured by CO2 as hydantoin and hydrolyzed via N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to polymeric nitrile material that hydrolyzes to amino acid precursor mixtures from which glycine is the dominant released amino acid.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry converts HCN feedstocks into aminoacetonitrile and formaldehyde-containing product sets, after which canonical hydrolysis yields glycine.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal alpha-keto acid chemistry supplies glyoxylate, and ferroan-brucite-like serpentinite chemistry converts glyoxylate plus nitrate directly into mineral-bound glycine.

pathways.name: Surface photocatalytic glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Glyoxylate derived from the hydrothermal alpha-keto acid pool is transported to surface photochemical settings where UV-driven semiconductor mineral chemistry aminates it to glycine.

pathways.name: Alanine-glyoxylate transamination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate undergoes reductive amination to alanine; biochemical transamination-like chemistry with glyoxylate yields glycine while regenerating pyruvate.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic-to-Bucherer route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile formed in the cyanosulfidic network is diverted by CO2 into hydantoin and then hydrolyzed through the Bucherer-Bergs sequence to glycine.

pathways.name: Direct atmospheric/surface Strecker from concentrated HCN and hydrothermal formaldehyde
pathways.description: This route emphasizes geochemical interconnection: atmospheric or eutectically concentrated HCN meets hydrothermally generated formaldehyde in surface ponds, giving aminoacetonitrile and ultimately glycine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong canonical Strecker route and best-aligned with the literature atlas. The aminoacetonitrile and glycinamide steps match the documented sequence well. Main weakness is the underspecified atmospheric/photochemical HCN generation under a CO2 + NH3 input set without clearer reducing equivalents or energy chemistry; hydrothermal formaldehyde from CO2/H2 is plausible but somewhat generalized.
suggestions: Specify a more literature-grounded HCN source, such as CO-containing atmospheric discharge or transient reducing atmospheric chemistry
Explicitly include aminomethanol as the imine precursor if a more mechanistic network is desired
State mineral agents and conditions for the hydrothermal formaldehyde-forming step to better satisfy environment-specific rigor

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A good Strecker branch emphasizing glycolonitrile, which is strongly supported in the literature as the dominant low-NH3 product from HCN/HCHO systems. Conversion of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile is plausible but more condition-sensitive than the canonical Strecker sequence and should be framed as dependent on ammonia availability and concentration.
suggestions: Clarify whether eutectic concentration or evaporative concentration enables productive glycolonitrile ammonolysis
Add conditions indicating pH and NH3 abundance for rxn_009
Tighten the environmental transfer description from hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface cyanohydrin chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a well-supported alternative to direct nitrile hydrolysis and aligns well with Taillades/Commeyras-type CO2-assisted hydantoin chemistry. The main issue is that the Bucherer-Bergs label is slightly broadened here because classical Bucherer-Bergs often invokes cyanide/carbonate chemistry from carbonyls, whereas this network uses aminoacetonitrile capture by CO2 as a reservoir route. Still, the hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine to glycine progression is coherent and plausible.
suggestions: Use bicarbonate/CO2 buffer language to align more closely with the cited room-temperature hydantoin literature
Clarify whether hydantoin formation is direct CO2 trapping of aminoacetonitrile or a shorthand for a broader carbonate-assisted network
Add explicit note that the hydrolysis steps are likely slow and favored in prolonged aqueous cycling

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The broad idea is literature-supported: HCN polymerization followed by hydrolysis can release glycine, often abundantly. However, the pathway is represented too generically. Product pools are chemically vague, and the network omits the concentration bottleneck emphasized by the literature: HCN polymerization generally requires high local concentration, often eutectic freezing or prolonged ammoniacal conditions.
suggestions: Specify eutectic freezing or concentrated NH4CN conditions for HCN polymer formation
Replace generic polymer hydrolysis pools with better-defined intermediates or explicitly mark them as unresolved mixture nodes
Distinguish acid versus base hydrolysis conditions leading to glycine release

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alignment with Patel et al.-style cyanosulfidic chemistry, especially in positioning aminoacetonitrile as a systems-chemistry output. The main concern is that the reaction summary is underspecified because the literature route depends on H2S, UV, phosphate buffer, and photoredox metal cycling, which are not explicit in the textual network summary here.
suggestions: Explicitly include H2S, UV, phosphate, and Cu photoredox context in the reaction definition
Clarify whether formaldehyde is generated in situ or imported from hydrothermal flux
Note that this route is a network chemistry route rather than a single clean transformation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The terminal glyoxylate + nitrate to glycine step is strongly aligned with the recent ferroan brucite literature. The major weakness is the upstream pyruvate to glyoxylate step, which is presented as a hydrothermal oxidative-retroconversion pool without strong support in the supplied literature. Because that step is weakly grounded, the pathway is less secure overall despite a strong final transformation.
suggestions: Provide a literature-based source of glyoxylate rather than relying on speculative pyruvate retroconversion
If retained, mark rxn_004 as speculative and define mineral/redox context explicitly
Highlight ferroan brucite product sequestration in the glycine-forming step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The glyoxylate-to-glycine amination under photochemical mineral mediation is literature-consistent in spirit, especially with ZnS-type photocatalysis, though the prompt's surface-mineral list omits ZnS. The weak point remains the unsupported origin of glyoxylate from pyruvate. As written, the final step is good but the pathway foundation is incomplete.
suggestions: Ground glyoxylate production in a cited route or import it from a better-supported source
Specify the photocatalyst and UV regime explicitly for rxn_020
Avoid overcommitting to pyruvate retroconversion unless directly supported

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway benefits from a strong alanine-forming hydrothermal step, which is well supported by iron oxyhydroxide chemistry, and a plausible transamination-like conversion of glyoxylate to glycine. It is somewhat more proto-metabolic than directly demonstrated prebiotic synthesis, and again depends on the least supported step in the network: pyruvate to glyoxylate.
suggestions: Describe rxn_021 as a transamination analog rather than implying full enzyme-like biochemistry
Strengthen or replace the glyoxylate source
Clarify whether mineral interfaces or repeated wet-dry/thermal cycling assist the amino transfer

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong hybrid pathway linking cyanosulfidic systems chemistry to hydantoin reservoir chemistry. This is chemically coherent and reflects the networked nature of prebiotic glycine synthesis. The same caveat applies as in P5 and P3: explicit cyanosulfidic conditions and more precise hydantoin-forming context would improve confidence.
suggestions: Make the sulfur/redox/phosphate context explicit for aminoacetonitrile generation
Specify bicarbonate or CO2-rich conditions for hydantoin capture
State whether this route is favored under low-NH3 conditions as suggested by the literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is effectively a well-connected cross-environment Strecker pathway and is among the best routes in the network. It appropriately uses atmospheric/surface HCN and hydrothermal formaldehyde. Its only substantive weakness is the simplified atmospheric HCN origin under a likely weakly reducing early atmosphere; the literature supports HCN best under transient reducing or CO-rich regimes.
suggestions: Tie HCN generation to CO-rich atmospheric discharge or localized volcanic lightning
Include concentration mechanisms such as evaporitic ponds or eutectic ice if surface inventory is dilute
Keep this route as the primary benchmark pathway because it best matches the literature

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is a strong, well-connected glycine synthesis network with clear hub structure, multiple convergent routes, and good overall alignment with the literature. Its greatest strengths are: strong emphasis on Strecker chemistry as the dominant route; explicit aminoacetonitrile and glycinamide intermediates; inclusion of Bucherer-Bergs and HCN polymer hydrolysis alternatives; and a credible hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical environmental flow. Hub choice is excellent: HCN, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, pyruvate, and glyoxylate all create dense connectivity and multiple independent routes to glycine. The target is clearly achieved by several chemically plausible pathways.

Main weaknesses are concentrated in a few steps rather than the network as a whole. First, several hydrothermal and surface reactions are underspecified relative to the prompt requirements: the summary provided here does not list explicit mineral agents for each hydrothermal/surface reaction, even though evaluation text implies they may exist in the full network. Second, atmospheric HCN production from CO2 and NH3 alone is too generic and should be framed in the context of transient reducing/CO-rich atmospheres, lightning, or photochemistry. Third, the pyruvate-to-glyoxylate conversion is the least literature-grounded transformation and weakens the glyoxylate-centered subnetwork. Fourth, the HCN polymer route is realistic only with explicit concentration mechanisms such as eutectic freezing or prolonged high-concentration ammoniacal conditions.

On literature alignment, the network performs well overall. It tracks the deep research closely: canonical Strecker chemistry, glycolonitrile branch, hydantoin/N-carbamoylglycine route, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile production, alanine formation from pyruvate under iron mediation, and ferroan-brucite-mediated glyoxylate+nitrate chemistry are all represented. The only notable misalignment is reliance on a weakly justified glyoxylate source and some overcompressed reaction notation for systems chemistry routes. Overall this is an approvable network with several excellent pathways, especially the Strecker-centered ones.
